title: Why Language Models Hallucinate
speaker: Adam Kalai
abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) sometimes generate statements that are plausible but factually incorrect—a phenomenon commonly called "hallucination." We argue that these errors are not mysterious failures of architecture or reasoning, but rather predictable consequences of standard training and evaluation incentives.
We show (i) that hallucinations can be viewed as classification errors: when pretrained models cannot reliably distinguish a false statement from a true one, they may produce the false option rather than saying I don't know; (ii) that optimization of benchmark performance encourages guessing rather than abstaining, since most evaluation metrics penalize expressing uncertainty; and (iii) that a possible mitigation path lies in revising existing benchmarks to reward calibrated abstention, thus realigning incentives in model development.
Joint work with Santosh Vempala (Georgia Tech) and Ofir Nachum & Edwin Zhang (OpenAI).

proof_stages.label: Hallucination as non-abstained falsehood
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines hallucination as false output where uncertainty or abstention would be preferable, motivates the safety problem, and separates hallucination rate from accuracy, model size, and inevitability misconceptions.

proof_stages.label: Idealized pretraining model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker decomposes training into language prediction and alignment, then formalizes pretraining as learning a distribution \(\hat p\) from noiseless positive samples supported on valid strings under the usual negative-log-likelihood objective.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from prediction to validity classification
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem repurposes any predictive model \(\hat p\) as a threshold classifier for valid versus erroneous strings and lower-bounds hallucination probability by roughly twice the classifier error minus small ratio and calibration terms.

proof_stages.label: Calibration induced by predictive loss
proof_stages.summary: The calibration term is argued to be small for models trained to minimize log loss, since miscalibration on heavy hitters would allow a rescaling that improves the objective, while degenerate non-hallucinating predictors fail this predictive calibration.

proof_stages.label: Sources and rates of pretraining hallucinations
proof_stages.summary: Standard reasons for binary-classification error—statistical unlearnability, absence of rules, and poor representations—are transferred to hallucinations, and a Good-Turing singleton estimate gives lower bounds for fact-like queries.

proof_stages.label: Post-training incentives that preserve hallucination
proof_stages.summary: Although alignment should remove pretraining hallucinations, common benchmarks and preference evaluations often score abstention like a wrong answer or reward confident-looking responses, making guessing the optimized behavior.

proof_stages.label: Penalty-aware evaluation and behavioral calibration
proof_stages.summary: The proposed remedy is to regrade existing evaluations with explicit wrong-answer penalties so models can choose when to abstain, yielding objective penalized scores and a behavioral-calibration notion measuring whether responses match the announced utility threshold.

proof_stages.label: Outlook on aligned objectives and robust aggregation
proof_stages.summary: The closing outlook frames AI safety as an engineering and theory problem, asks what aligned models should be trained to say, and presents a median-distribution aggregation theorem showing how a majority of safe model distributions can amplify safety up to an overlap factor.
